## Background — why this redesign (read first)
Read `analogies/l1-bridge.md` (just written by a Mathlib-analogist) in full. It establishes that the
L1 categorical→module bridge for `lem:cech_acyclic_affine`, **as currently stated** (vanishing of the

affine-pushforward exactness is absent from Mathlib (its "Q4", the decisive blocker).

**The planner has decided (Q4):** re-state `lem:cech_acyclic_affine` to vanish the **absolute section
Čech complex** `Č•(𝒰, F)` = `def:section_cech_complex` (`AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplex`, already

- On the standard cover by basic opens `D(s_i)` of `Spec R`, the section-complex terms are
  `F(⨅_k D(s_{σ k})) = F(D(∏_k s_{σ k})) = F(D(s_σ)) = M_{s_σ}` (away-localisation), so the localisation
  route (`exact_of_isLocalized_span` + the done `CombinatorialCech.Dependent.depDiff_exact`) applies

  **section Čech complex** of `def:section_cech_complex` for the standard affine cover: for

  `Ideal.span (Set.range s) = ⊤` (finite `ι`), and `p ≥ 1`,
  `Ȟᵖ(𝒰, F) = 0` where `𝒰` is the standard cover by `D(s_i)` and `Ȟᵖ` is the cohomology of the
  **section** complex `Č•(𝒰, F)`.

1. **`def:qcoh_sections_localized`** (section = localisation; the analogist's Q1). For quasi-coherent
   `F` on `Spec R` and `g : R`, the sections `F(D(g))` form the away-localisation `M_g` of
   `M = Γ(Spec R, F)`; concretely `IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) (restriction map)`. Note the
   analogist's gap: Mathlib has `IsLocalizedModule … (tilde.toOpen M (basicOpen g)).hom` and
   `tilde.toOpen_res` for `tilde M`, but the step `F ≅ tilde (Γ F)` for an arbitrary quasi-coherent
   `F` needs globalising local `QuasicoherentData` (Stacks 01I8, ~100–150 LOC project-side). State
   this as a sub-lemma to build; cite `references/stacks-schemes.tex` 01HV(4)–(5) and the gap.

3. The **assembly**: feed each positive-degree node through `exact_of_isLocalized_span (Set.range s) hs`

64	# Blueprint Writer
65

67

73

75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78

81

83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex

97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```

108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}

118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."

123	\end{theorem}

127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."

130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels

136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147

You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechAcyclic.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/PresheafCech.lean
6	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FreePresheafComplex.lean
7	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechBridge.lean
8	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
9	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
10	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
11	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
12	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
13	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
14	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	
18	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
19	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
20	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
22	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
23	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
24	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
25	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
26	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
27	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
28	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
29	
30	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
31	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
32	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
33	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
34	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
35	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
36	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
37	\[
38	  \begin{array}{ccc}
39	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
40	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
41	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
42	  \end{array}
43	\]
44	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
45	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
46	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
47	
48	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
54	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
56	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
57	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
58	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
59	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
60	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
61	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
62	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
63	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
64	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
65	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
66	  % the unit ideal, see
67	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
68	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
69	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
70	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
71	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
72	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
73	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
74	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
75	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
76	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
77	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
78	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
79	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
80	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
81	  \[
82	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
83	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
84	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
85	  \]
86	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
87	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
88	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
89	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
90	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
91	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
92	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
93	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
94	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
95	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
96	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
97	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
98	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
99	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).

166	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
167	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
168	
169	It is convenient to factor the construction of
170	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
171	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
172	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
173	
174	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
175	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
176	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
177	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
178	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
179	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
180	\[
181	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
182	    \;=\;
183	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
184	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
185	\]
186	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
187	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
188	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
189	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
190	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
191	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
192	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
193	
194	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
195	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
196	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
197	\[
198	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
199	  \qquad
200	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
201	\]
202	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
203	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
204	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
205	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
206	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
207	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
208	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
209	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
210	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
211	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
212	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
213	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
214	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
215	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
216	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
217	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
218	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
219	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
220	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
221	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
222	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
223	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
224	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
225	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
226	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
227	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
228	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
229	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
230	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
231	
232	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
233	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
234	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
235	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
236	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
237	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
238	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
239	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
240	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
241	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
242	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
243	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
244	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
245	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
246	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
247	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
248	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
249	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
250	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
251	
252	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
253	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
254	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
255	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
256	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
257	
258	\begin{definition}\leanok

263	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
264	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
265	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
266	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery

299	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
300	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
302	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
303	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
304	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
305	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
306	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
307	  \[
308	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
309	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
310	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
311	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
312	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
313	      \;=\;
314	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
315	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
316	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
317	  \]
318	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
319	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
320	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
321	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
322	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
323	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
324	\end{definition}

332	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
333	\end{lemma}

336	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
337	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
338	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
339	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
340	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
341	\end{proof}

344	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
345	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
347	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
348	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
349	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
350	  \[
351	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
352	  \]
353	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
354	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
355	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
356	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
357	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.

361	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
362	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
363	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
364	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
365	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
366	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
367	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
368	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
369	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
370	\end{proof}

374	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}

377	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a

600	  \paragraph{Identification of the abstract {\v C}ech complex with the localisation
601	  complex (the categorical--module bridge).}
602	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
603	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691--719).
604	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
605	  % as an $R_f$-module.
606	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
607	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
608	  % are the maps $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
609	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
610	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
611	  items (4)--(5).}
612	  The {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is built from the
613	  \emph{abstract} cover \(\mathcal{U} = (\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\;
614	  s\; hs).\mathrm{openCover}\) of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}, whose
615	  degree-\(p\) term is an object of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} A)\); we
616	  record explicitly how those terms and their differential become the concrete
617	  complex of localised modules that the homotopy argument operates on (writing
618	  \(s_i = f_i\) for the spanning family). First, the \(i\)-th piece of
619	  \(\mathcal{U}\) is \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_{s_i}) \to \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the
620	  open immersion of the basic open \(D(s_i)\); the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection
621	  indexed by a tuple \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) is the basic open
622	  \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
623	  \[
624	    s_\sigma \;=\; \prod_{k=0}^{p} s_{\sigma(k)}
625	      \;=\; s_{\sigma(0)} \cdots s_{\sigma(p)},
626	  \]
627	  because the basic opens of an affine satisfy \(D(s_{i_0}) \cap \cdots \cap
628	  D(s_{i_p}) = D(s_{i_0} \cdots s_{i_p})\); its sections form the \emph{away}
629	  localisation \(A_{s_\sigma} = A_{s_{\sigma(0)} \cdots s_{\sigma(p)}}\). Second,
630	  because \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent, item~(4) of the cited
631	  lemma identifies its sections over each basic open with the away localisation of
632	  the module,
633	  \[
634	    \Gamma\bigl(D(s_\sigma), \mathcal{F}\bigr) \;=\; M_{s_\sigma},
635	  \]
636	  the localisation of \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by the product
637	  \(s_\sigma\) (the \(\operatorname{Localization.Away}\) /
638	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) localisation of \(M\) at \(s_\sigma\)). Hence
639	  the degree-\(p\) term of the abstract {\v C}ech complex is, term by term, the
640	  product of away localisations
641	  \[
642	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
643	      \;=\; \prod_{\sigma : \{0,\ldots,p\} \to \iota}
644	              \Gamma\bigl(D(s_\sigma), \mathcal{F}\bigr)
645	      \;=\; \prod_{\sigma : \{0,\ldots,p\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma}.
646	  \]
647	  Third, the {\v C}ech differential of the abstract complex --- the alternating sum
648	  \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1}(-1)^j\) of the restriction maps along the face inclusions
649	  \(D(s_\sigma) \hookrightarrow D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})\) obtained by omitting the
650	  \(j\)-th index (so \(D(s_\sigma) \subset D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})\), the product
651	  \(s_\sigma\) carrying the extra factor \(s_{\sigma(j)}\)) --- is, by item~(5) of
652	  the cited lemma (the restriction of \(\widetilde M\) along an inclusion of basic
653	  opens \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is precisely the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)),
654	  exactly the alternating sum of the localisation maps
655	  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) between the away localisations. The
656	  degreewise identification of the second step therefore intertwines the abstract
657	  {\v C}ech coboundary with the concrete localised coboundary: it is an isomorphism
658	  of cochain complexes from \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
659	  onto the concrete complex of away localisations. Consequently the positive-degree
660	  vanishing \(\operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)\)
661	  of the abstract complex is equivalent to positive-degree exactness of the concrete
662	  localised complex, and it is the latter that the local-to-global criterion and the
663	  contracting homotopy below establish.
664	
665	  By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
666	  the standard cover is therefore the complex of localisations
667	  \[
668	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
669	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
670	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
671	  \]
672	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
673	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
674	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}).
675	  Exactness may be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime
676	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\) (Algebra, Lemma
677	  \texttt{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}). Choose an index
678	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
679	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
680	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
681	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
682	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
683	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
684	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
685	  \(p > 0\).
686	
687	  Concretely, exactness is established node-by-node from the spanning family
688	  \((s, hs)\) of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}: the local-to-global
689	  exactness criterion for a span of the unit ideal localises the complex at the
690	  spanning elements (the away localisations \(A_{s_r}\)), reducing positive-degree
691	  exactness of the extended complex to exactness of each localised complex; in the
692	  localisation \(A_{s_r}\) the fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes
693	  \(s_r\) invertible, so the contracting homotopy
694	  \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{r\, i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is defined globally on that
695	  localisation and contracts it.
696	
697	  \paragraph{The categorical--module bridge (L1), as a formalizable infrastructure
698	  decomposition.}
699	  The combinatorial contracting homotopy above and its consequence
700	  \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) are purely formal once the abstract {\v C}ech
701	  complex has been transported to the concrete cochain complex of localised
702	  \(A\)-modules; the substantive remaining work is exactly that transport, which we
703	  now decompose into three independent pieces, each a self-contained statement a
704	  module-level argument can discharge.
705	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
706	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L692--707).
707	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
708	  % as an $R_f$-module.
709	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
710	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
711	  % are the maps $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
712	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
713	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
714	  items (4)--(5).}
715	
716	  \emph{(1) Section identification.} For the standard cover by basic opens
717	  \(D(s_\sigma)\) with \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), the sections of
718	  \(\operatorname{pushPullObj} \mathcal{F}\) over the basic open
719	  \(D(s_\sigma) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} R\) are the away localisation
720	  \(M_{s_\sigma}\) of \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\). The
721	  module-level input is Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the
722	  instance \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\)
723	  of the comparison map \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) and
724	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) (that \(g\) acts invertibly on
725	  the sections over \(D(g)\)); quasicoherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) connects
726	  \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(\widetilde{M}\). By item~(4) of Tag~01HV,
727	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\) as an \(R_g\)-module, and by item~(5) the
728	  restriction along \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\).
729	
730	  \emph{(2) Differential identification.} Under (1), the abstract
731	  \(\operatorname{relativeCechComplexOfNerve}\) / cosimplicial differential becomes the
732	  alternating localisation coboundary, i.e.\ the concrete \(\delta\) feeding
733	  \(\operatorname{depDiff}\) of the dependent combinatorial port. The bridge must
734	  discharge three compatibility hypotheses, which are exactly the interface of that
735	  dependent port: \(\mathtt{hu}\) (unit: \(c \circ \delta_0 = \operatorname{id}\)),
736	  \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (shift: \(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)), and
737	  \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) (coface commutation, that the localisation cofaces commute as
738	  required by the cosimplicial identities). Here \(c\) is the
739	  prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) transport (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) and the
740	  \(\delta_k\) are the localisation cofaces.
741	
742	  \emph{(3) Homology \(\leftrightarrow\) exactness.} The goal
743	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\bigl((\operatorname{CechComplex} \ldots).\mathrm{homology}\,
744	  p\bigr)\) reduces, via the sectionwise identification on the affine base of (1)--(2),
745	  to \(\operatorname{Function.Exact} d^{p-1}\, d^{p}\) of the underlying \(R\)-module
746	  cochain complex. Each positive-degree node is fed through
747	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\, hs\),
748	  localising at the spanning elements (the \(\operatorname{Away}(s_r)\)) so that
749	  exactness is checked one localisation at a time; in the localisation at \(s_r\) the
750	  fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes the prepend homotopy global, and the node
751	  is closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent port. The constant
752	  and dependent combinatorial cores (the \((dh+hd) = \operatorname{id}\) identity in
753	  both the constant-coefficient and the dependent-coefficient settings) and the
754	  spanning-family certifier are already in place; (1)--(3) are the only remaining
755	  infrastructure.
756	
757	  This bridge is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
758	  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
759	  extra-degeneracy packaging of the contracting homotopy is a documented dead end for
760	  this cover (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map is only a section-level homotopy
761	  after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra degeneracy), so the concrete
762	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
763	  is used instead.
764	\end{proof}

We summarize the results obtained so far.

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-spec-sheaves}
Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
associated to $M$.
\begin{enumerate}
\item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R$.
\item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \widetilde M) = M$ as an $R$-module.
\item For every $f \in R$ we have
$\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R_f$.
\item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
as an $R_f$-module.
\item Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
are the maps
$R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
\ref{lemma-standard-open}.
\item Let $\mathfrak p$ be a prime of $R$, and let $x \in \Spec(R)$
be the corresponding point. We have
$\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R), x} = R_{\mathfrak p}$.
\item Let $\mathfrak p$ be a prime of $R$, and let $x \in \Spec(R)$
be the corresponding point. We have $\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}$
as an $R_{\mathfrak p}$-module.
\end{enumerate}
Moreover, all these identifications are functorial in the $R$
module $M$. In particular, the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde M$
is an exact functor from the category of $R$-modules
to the category of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules.
\end{lemma}

Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). The cover is presented as the
  spanning-family bundle \((s : \iota \to A,\; hs : \operatorname{Ideal.span}
  (\operatorname{range} s) = \top)\) of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}
  rather than as a general open cover, so the index set and the affine pieces
  \(D(s_i)\) with sections \(A_{s_i}\) are read off directly from the spanning
  family. Then the {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} has vanishing cohomology in all positive
  degrees:
  \[
    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
  \]
  Equivalently, the extended complex
  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}}
  \to \cdots\) of localisations is exact. This is the standard-cover {\v C}ech
  vanishing only; the sheaf-cohomology Serre vanishing
  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) is the separate
  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} below, obtained from this complex
  vanishing by the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.
\end{lemma}

Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) the associated
  affine scheme, and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
  (\(\mathcal{F} : (\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Modules}\)). Let
  \(s : \iota \to R\) (\(\iota\) finite) be a spanning family of the unit ideal,
  \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\), and let
  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in \iota} D(s_i)\) be the associated standard
  affine cover of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}; the index set and the
  affine pieces \(D(s_i)\) with sections \(R_{s_i}\) are read off directly from the
  spanning family. The statement is \emph{intrinsic} to \(\operatorname{Spec} R\):
  there is no relative morphism \(f\) and no pushforward in the conclusion. Then the
  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- the cochain
  complex of abelian groups whose degree-\(p\) term is
  \(\prod_{\sigma}\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) --- has vanishing cohomology in all
  positive degrees:
  \[
    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0,
  \]
  where \(\check{H}^p\) denotes the cohomology of the section complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\). Equivalently, the
  extended complex
  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1}}
  \to \cdots\) of localisations of \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\)
  is exact in positive degrees. This is the standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing
  only; the sheaf-cohomology Serre vanishing \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) is the
  separate Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} below, obtained from this complex
  vanishing by the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.
\end{lemma}

\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}

The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.

\begin{definition}
[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}
  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
  % as an $R_f$-module.
  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
  % are the maps
  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
  items (4)--(5).}
  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).

  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[Section {\v C}ech homology vanishes iff the localised complex is exact]
  \label{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact}
  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
  % L58--67).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
  % is identified with the complex
  % $$
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
  % \ldots
  % $$"
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
  complex of localisations).}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
  \[
    D^\bullet : \quad
    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
    \cdots,
    \qquad
    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
  \]
  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. For
  each \(p\), vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
  underlying \(R\)-module maps,
  \[
    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr)
    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
    \operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr).
  \]
  The two directions are supplied by Mathlib: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the
  homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at \(p\)
  (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)), and \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
  of a short complex of \(R\)-modules is \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the
  underlying linear maps (\(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\)).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
  differential of \(D^\bullet\). This gives the isomorphism of cochain complexes
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong D^\bullet\). The
  homology equivalence then follows by transporting along this isomorphism and
  applying \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) (\(\operatorname{IsZero}\)
  homology \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)) and
  \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) (\(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
  \(R\)-modules \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\)).

  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
  being proved.
\end{proof}

  open immersion of the basic open \(D(s_i)\); the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection

  \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
  \[
    s_\sigma \;=\; \prod_{k=0}^{p} s_{\sigma(k)}
      \;=\; s_{\sigma(0)} \cdots s_{\sigma(p)},
  \]
  because the basic opens of an affine satisfy \(D(s_{i_0}) \cap \cdots \cap
  D(s_{i_p}) = D(s_{i_0} \cdots s_{i_p})\); its sections form the \emph{away}
  localisation \(A_{s_\sigma} = A_{s_{\sigma(0)} \cdots s_{\sigma(p)}}\). Second,
  because \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent, item~(4) of the cited
  lemma identifies its sections over each basic open with the away localisation of
  the module,
  \[
    \Gamma\bigl(D(s_\sigma), \mathcal{F}\bigr) \;=\; M_{s_\sigma},
  \]
  the localisation of \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by the product
  \(s_\sigma\) (the \(\operatorname{Localization.Away}\) /
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) localisation of \(M\) at \(s_\sigma\)). Hence
  the degree-\(p\) term of the abstract {\v C}ech complex is, term by term, the
  product of away localisations

    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
      \;=\; \prod_{\sigma : \{0,\ldots,p\} \to \iota}
              \Gamma\bigl(D(s_\sigma), \mathcal{F}\bigr)
      \;=\; \prod_{\sigma : \{0,\ldots,p\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma}.
  \]
  Third, the {\v C}ech differential of the abstract complex --- the alternating sum
  \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1}(-1)^j\) of the restriction maps along the face inclusions
  \(D(s_\sigma) \hookrightarrow D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})\) obtained by omitting the
  \(j\)-th index (so \(D(s_\sigma) \subset D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})\), the product

  exactly the alternating sum of the localisation maps
  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) between the away localisations. The
  degreewise identification of the second step therefore intertwines the abstract
  {\v C}ech coboundary with the concrete localised coboundary: it is an isomorphism
  of cochain complexes from \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
  onto the concrete complex of away localisations. Consequently the positive-degree
  vanishing \(\operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)\)
  of the abstract complex is equivalent to positive-degree exactness of the concrete
  localised complex, and it is the latter that the local-to-global criterion and the
  contracting homotopy below establish.

  By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
  the standard cover is therefore the complex of localisations
  \[
    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
  \]

  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\) (Algebra, Lemma
  \texttt{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}). Choose an index
  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then

  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
  \(p > 0\).

  \(s_r\) invertible, so the contracting homotopy
  \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{r\, i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is defined globally on that
  localisation and contracts it.

  \paragraph{The categorical--module bridge (L1), as a formalizable infrastructure
  decomposition.}
  The combinatorial contracting homotopy above and its consequence
  \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) are purely formal once the abstract {\v C}ech
  complex has been transported to the concrete cochain complex of localised
  \(A\)-modules; the substantive remaining work is exactly that transport, which we
  now decompose into three independent pieces, each a self-contained statement a
  module-level argument can discharge.

  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L692--707).

  % are the maps $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
  items (4)--(5).}

  \emph{(1) Section identification.} For the standard cover by basic opens
  \(D(s_\sigma)\) with \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), the sections of
  \(\operatorname{pushPullObj} \mathcal{F}\) over the basic open
  \(D(s_\sigma) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} R\) are the away localisation
  \(M_{s_\sigma}\) of \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\). The
  module-level input is Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the
  instance \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\)
  of the comparison map \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) and
  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) (that \(g\) acts invertibly on
  the sections over \(D(g)\)); quasicoherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) connects

  \(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\) as an \(R_g\)-module, and by item~(5) the
  restriction along \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\).

  \emph{(2) Differential identification.} Under (1), the abstract
  \(\operatorname{relativeCechComplexOfNerve}\) / cosimplicial differential becomes the
  alternating localisation coboundary, i.e.\ the concrete \(\delta\) feeding
  \(\operatorname{depDiff}\) of the dependent combinatorial port. The bridge must
  discharge three compatibility hypotheses, which are exactly the interface of that
  dependent port: \(\mathtt{hu}\) (unit: \(c \circ \delta_0 = \operatorname{id}\)),
  \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (shift: \(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)), and
  \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) (coface commutation, that the localisation cofaces commute as
  required by the cosimplicial identities). Here \(c\) is the
  prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) transport (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) and the
  \(\delta_k\) are the localisation cofaces.

  \emph{(3) Homology \(\leftrightarrow\) exactness.} The goal
  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\bigl((\operatorname{CechComplex} \ldots).\mathrm{homology}\,
  p\bigr)\) reduces, via the sectionwise identification on the affine base of (1)--(2),

  cochain complex. Each positive-degree node is fed through

  localising at the spanning elements (the \(\operatorname{Away}(s_r)\)) so that

  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the

  this cover (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map is only a section-level homotopy
  after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra degeneracy), so the concrete

\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_homology_exact, AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.depDiff_exact}
  Write \(R\) for the ring, \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for the
  global sections, and \((s : \iota \to R,\; hs : \operatorname{Ideal.span}
  (\operatorname{range} s) = \top)\) for the spanning family of
  Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}.

  \paragraph{Reduction to the localised complex.}
  By the section identification of Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, each
  \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap D(s_{\sigma(p)})\)
  is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
  \(s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)}\), and its sections are the away localisation
  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)) = M_{s_\sigma}\); under these identifications each face
  restriction is the canonical localisation map. Hence
  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} identifies the section {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} with the concrete complex of localised
  \(R\)-modules
  \[
    \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \;\to\;
    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1}} \;\to\;
    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1} s_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
  \]
  and reduces the vanishing
  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\) for \(p > 0\) to positive-degree
  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying localisation maps. Equivalently,
  the extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is
  exact in positive degrees.

  \paragraph{Node-by-node exactness via local-to-global and the dependent port.}
  Exactness of each positive-degree node is established from the spanning family
  \((s, hs)\). The local-to-global exactness criterion
  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\, hs\)
  reduces \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p-1}, d^{p})\) to exactness after
  localising at each spanning element \(r\) (the away localisation
  \(\operatorname{Away}(s_r)\)), checking exactness one localisation at a time. In the
  localisation at \(s_r\) the fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes \(s_r\)
  invertible, so the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) transport \(c\)
  (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) is defined globally on that localisation and
  furnishes a contracting homotopy of the localised complex: the unit, shift, and
  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\) (\(c \circ \delta_0 =
  \operatorname{id}\)), \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (\(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)),
  and \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) hold for the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\). The node is
  then closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent combinatorial port,
  which packages the identity \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) into the
  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) statement that
  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}\) consumes. The constant- and
  dependent-coefficient combinatorial cores and the spanning-family certifier are
  already in place; the section and differential identifications of
  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and the homology--exactness reduction of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} are the bridge that connects them to the
  abstract section complex.

  The homotopy is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
  extra-degeneracy packaging of the contracting homotopy is a documented dead end for
  this cover (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map is only a section-level homotopy
  after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra degeneracy), so the concrete
  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
  is used instead.
\end{proof}
